one_liner: A 35-year-old female with no significant past medical history presents with a productive cough for 10 days following an upper respiratory infection.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient developed nasal congestion, sore throat, and malaise 2 weeks ago, consistent with a viral URI. While the nasal congestion and sore throat improved, she developed a persistent productive cough 10 days ago that has not resolved. The cough produces yellowish-green sputum and is worse at night, sometimes disrupting her sleep. She reports mild substernal chest discomfort with coughing. She has had a low-grade temperature measured at 99.8°F at home. She has tried over-the-counter cough suppressants and honey with minimal relief. She denies high fevers (>101°F), shortness of breath at rest, hemoptysis, wheezing, or pleuritic chest pain. She has no history of asthma, COPD, or recurrent this condition. She denies sick contacts with influenza or COVID-19.
stage2.medications: Loratadine 10 mg daily as needed for allergies
stage2.family_history: No family history of asthma, COPD, or lung disease.
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol. Works as an elementary school teacher (frequent exposure to childhood illnesses). Lives with husband and one child. No recent travel. Child had a cold 3 weeks ago.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Well-appearing woman in no acute distress, occasionally coughing during the exam**. HEENT: **Mild nasal erythema, clear nasal discharge, oropharynx with mild posterior pharyngeal cobblestoning (postnasal drip), no tonsillar exudates or enlargement**; **Tympanic membranes normal**. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: **Scattered coarse rhonchi bilaterally that clear partially with coughing**; No wheezes; No crackles; No dullness to percussion; **Good air entry bilaterally**. Abdomen: **Benign**.

lab_results.result: WBC 8,200/μL (55% neutrophils, 35% lymphocytes), Hgb 13.5 g/dL, Plt 240,000/μL
lab_results.clinical_significance: Normal WBC count without left shift; makes bacterial pneumonia less likely
Mildly elevated CRP common in viral infections;

imaging_results.result: Clear lung fields bilaterally. No infiltrates, consolidation, or effusions. No hilar lymphadenopathy. Normal cardiac silhouette.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: this condition is a clinical diagnosis that does not show infiltrates on imaging

acceptable_tests: Pertussis PCR (if cough >2 weeks with paroxysms)

stage5.final_clue: The patient returns 2 weeks later and reports that her cough has significantly improved over the past week and is now only occasional and dry. This natural resolution timeline (cough lasting up to 3 weeks total) is characteristic of No antibiotics were prescribed.

debrief.classic_presentation: Acute bronchitis typically presents with a cough lasting 1-3 weeks, often following an upper respiratory infection. The cough may be productive with discolored (yellow or green) sputum — purulent sputum does NOT indicate bacterial infection and is caused by inflammatory cells (neutrophils). Patients may have low-grade fever, substernal chest discomfort with coughing, and rhonchi on auscultation. The lungs should be clear of focal crackles or consolidation. Chest X-ray is normal. It is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out pneumonia.
debrief.pathophysiology: Acute bronchitis is inflammation of the tracheobronchial tree, caused by viral infection in >90% of cases. Common viral etiologies include rhinovirus, influenza, parainfluenza, respiratory syncytial virus, adenovirus, and coronavirus. The virus damages bronchial epithelium, triggering an inflammatory response with mucus hypersecretion, mucosal edema, and impaired mucociliary clearance. This produces the characteristic cough and sputum production. The purulent (discolored) appearance of sputum reflects sloughed epithelial cells and neutrophils, NOT bacterial infection. Bronchial hyperreactivity can persist for weeks after the acute infection, explaining the prolonged cough.
debrief.treatment_overview: Treatment is supportive: (1) Symptomatic relief — honey (1-2 tablespoons, proven benefit), dextromethorphan or guaifenesin for cough, acetaminophen/ibuprofen for fever and discomfort, adequate hydration; (2) Antibiotics are NOT indicated — multiple RCTs and Cochrane reviews show minimal to no benefit in uncomplicated acute bronchitis, and antibiotic overuse contributes to resistance; (3) Short-acting bronchodilators (albuterol inhaler) may help if significant wheezing or bronchospasm is present; (4) Patient education: explain that cough may last 2-3 weeks and that green/yellow sputum does NOT mean bacterial infection; (5) Return if: high fever develops, hemoptysis, worsening dyspnea, symptoms beyond 3 weeks, or new concerning symptoms. Consider pertussis testing if cough >2 weeks with paroxysms, post-tussive vomiting, or inspiratory whoop.
high_yield_pearls: Antibiotics are NOT indicated for acute bronchitis — over 90% of cases are viral; purulent sputum does NOT indicate bacterial infection
Procalcitonin <0.25 ng/mL strongly supports withholding antibiotics and can reduce unnecessary prescriptions
The cough of acute bronchitis typically lasts 2-3 weeks; if it persists beyond 3 weeks, consider pertussis, post-infectious cough, or asthma
Acute bronchitis is the most common condition for which antibiotics are inappropriately prescribed in outpatient settings
sources: AAFP: Appropriate Antibiotic Use for Acute Respiratory Tract Infection in Adults 2024

after_stage2_more_likely: Acute bronchitis (productive cough following URI, no high fever, rhonchi that clear with cough, normal oxygen saturation)
after_stage2_less_likely: Pneumonia (no focal crackles, no high fever, no dyspnea at rest)
Asthma (no history, no wheezing, no recurrent episodes)
